section_path: SUBMISSION BY DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN – NETHERLANDS ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
paragraphs.text: Defence for Children Netherlands welcomes the European Union’s commitment to protecting children in the digital technology and supports the Digital Fairness Act (DFA), which we hope will meaningfully address the current gaps in the protection of children online. As a child rights organisation, we stress that DFA must be grounded in the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC) and ensure that children are not treated merely as consumers but as rights-holders in digital technology.

section_path: SUBMISSION BY DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN – NETHERLANDS ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
paragraphs.text: Today, one in three internet users is a child, and children are going online at younger ages and spending more time on digital devices than ever before. Digital technology is an integral part of children’s lives. It allows them to connect with others, access information and learn in new ways. It offers opportunities to develop skills, express creativity through social media, strengthen focus through gaming, build friendships, and experience a sense of belonging and inclusion. It also introduces new forms of play that broaden their horizons.

section_path: SUBMISSION BY DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN – NETHERLANDS ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
paragraphs.text: At the same time, digital technology can seriously undermine children’s rights and well-being. Research consistently shows that digital design practices can negatively affect children’s cognitive, emotional and physical development. Problematic or dysfunctional smartphone use, mirroring addictive behaviour, is increasingly common. Evidence also suggests that excessive social media use contributes to the growing incidence of mental health problems among adolescents, with social media pressure recognised as one of the leading causes of psychological distress in minors.

section_path: SUBMISSION BY DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN – NETHERLANDS ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
paragraphs.text: There are growing concerns that certain digital services are built around manipulative or profit-driven design features (so-called dark patterns), which may exploit children’s developmental vulnerabilities.

Defence for Children – ECPAT the Netherlands promotes the rights of the child in the Netherlands and worldwide, by providing information on the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child, investigating violations of these rights and protecting the collective and individual interests of children. Through our Children’s Rights Helpdesk, we provide legal support when children’s rights are (threatened to) be violated and we bring violations under the attention of the public and policy makers. In the Netherlands, Defence for Children is part of two international networks: Defence for Children International and ECPAT International.
Defence for Children - ECPAT NL Wafaa Abdelhadi & Celine Verheijen T 071 5160980 w.abdelhadi@defenceforchildren.nl c.verheijen@defenceforchildren-ecpat.nl

Persuasive and addictive design techniques, such as infinite scrolling, autoplay and reward mechanisms, encourage prolonged engagement, often at the expense of children’s concentration, sleep and mental health. These design-driven risks blur the boundaries between play, social interaction and commerce, fostering anxiety, compulsive use and loss of autonomy.

In line with the UNCRC, children’s rights to freedom of expression, education, play and participation must be supported in digital spaces just as offline. The UN Committee on the Rights of the Child’s General Comment No. 25 (2021) affirmed that children’s rights apply equally online and offline, rejecting any notion that the internet is an exception. Children have the right to be protected offline from manipulation and exploitation, and they must be afforded the same level of protection online, recognising that the online and offline dimensions of their lives are deeply interconnected and continuously shape each other, influencing children’s well-being, development and the full realisation of their rights.
This consultation response proceeds from that child rights perspective, weighing how the DFA can maximize the digital technology’s benefits for children while minimizing its dangers.

section_path: GAPS IN THE CURRENT EU FRAMEWORK
paragraphs.text: The current EU legislation and regulation, including the Digital Services Act (DSA), General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), provide valuable safeguards but remain fragmented and largely designed around the notion of an adult “average consumer.” These frameworks do not sufficiently address children’s developmental vulnerabilities or the ways in which digital design and commercial systems exploit their attention, emotions and data. The GDPR recognises children as vulnerable data subjects and provides higher consent standards, but it does not address behavioural design or attention-maximising systems that affect children’s well-being beyond data processing. The UCPD acknowledges that children merit special protection, but it was drafted in a pre-social media era and does not capture new forms of digital manipulation, such as influencer advertising, loot boxes and gamified in-app purchases.

section_path: GAPS IN THE CURRENT EU FRAMEWORK
paragraphs.text: The DSA introduced important duties for online platforms to moderate illegal content and protect minors, such as banning targeted advertising based on children’s data. Yet its scope is narrow as it covers mainly intermediary services and very large platforms and focuses on content moderation rather than manipulative or addictive design. Many unfair practices, including those embedded in games, apps, or influencer marketing, are all outside its reach.

section_path: GAPS IN THE CURRENT EU FRAMEWORK

section_path: GAPS IN THE CURRENT EU FRAMEWORK
paragraphs.text: DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN

section_path: GAPS IN THE CURRENT EU FRAMEWORK
paragraphs.text: DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN IS LID VAN ECPAT INTERNATIONAL

section_path: GAPS IN THE CURRENT EU FRAMEWORK
paragraphs.text: Children's rights and children's needs and developmental vulnerabilities are still not systematically reflected in the design and enforcement of digital market rules. This gap underlines the need for a more coherent and child-centred approach across all relevant digital and consumer laws, ensuring that fairness, safety and well-being are built in from the outset.

When it comes to the protection of children, the DFA must be firmly grounded in the UNCRC and must therefore reflect a rights-based approach that upholds their right to protection, participation and development.

The four general principles of the UNCRC offer a framework for all measures affecting children in the digital sphere: Non-discrimination (Article 2); Best interests of the child as a primary consideration (Article 3); Right to life, survival and development (Article 6); Right to respect for the views of the child (Article 12). These principles must guide how digital technologies are designed, regulated, and used. The UN Committee on the Rights of the Child has made clear that these rights apply equally online and offline, and that states and businesses share a proactive duty to protect and uphold children's rights in the digital environment.
Several provisions of the UNCRC articles provide concrete guidance for shaping the DFA:
Article 17 – the right of access to appropriate information: digital services must provide children with safe, age-appropriate and beneficial content while shielding them from harmful material;
Article 24 – the right to health: platforms must avoid features that undermine children's physical or mental well-being;
Article 31 – the right to rest, play and leisure: digital products should foster balance, play, and creative exploration;

Article 36 – the right to protection from economic exploitation: children must be safeguarded from manipulative monetization strategies such as loot boxes, hidden in-app purchases, or influencer-driven pressure to spend. Such practices are inherent to the business models of many online platforms and can lead to economic, physical and mental harm.

Children's rights must be systematically embedded in both the design and enforcement of the DFA. The regulation should explicitly state that where commercial and children's interests are in conflict, the best interests of the child must prevail, in accordance with Article 3 of the UNCRC.

section_path: FAIRNESS BY DESIGN AND ETHICAL DEFAULTS
paragraphs.text: The DFA must require that fairness is designed into digital products and not retrofitted after harm occurs. In line with Article 3 of the UNCRC, the best interests of the child must be a primary consideration in how platforms are built, monetised and

Four articles in the Convention are seen as special. They're known as the "General Principles" and they help to interpret all the other articles and play a fundamental role in realizing all the rights in the Convention for all children. They are:
Non-discrimination (Art. 2)
Best interest of the child (Art. 3)
Life, survival and development (Art. 6)
Right to be heard (Art. 12)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: In theory, all the children's rights in the Children's Rights Convention also apply in the digital world. Yet some are more applicable than others to digital technologies:

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Non-discrimination (Art. 2)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Best interest of the child (Art. 3)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Life, survival and development (Art. 6)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Respect for the views of the child (Art. 12)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Freedom of expression (Art. 13)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Freedom of association (Art. 15)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Right to privacy (Art. 16)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Access to information (Art. 17)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Parental responsibilities and state assistance (Art. 18)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Right to education (Art. 28)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Leisure, play and culture (Art. 31)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Protection against sexual abuse and (economic) exploitation (Arts. 34 - 36)

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: experienced. Design choices that exploit children's attention, emotions or developmental vulnerabilities should be considered incompatible with this obligation. The DFA must therefore prohibit manipulative and addictive practices including dark patterns, autoplay, infinite scroll and psychologically exploitative reward systems. These features are engineered to maximise engagement and commercial gain, often at the cost of children's well-being, health and autonomy. As such, they risk violating children's rights under Articles 6, 24 and 36 UNCRC the right to development, to the highest attainable standard of health, and to protection from being exploited economically.

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: In addition, ethical design must become the default. Default settings should prioritise safety, privacy and balance.[16] For example, addictive features should be turned off by default. Children should never have to navigate complex settings to access their rights. Platforms must put vulnerable user in control not design them into patterns of overuse or commercial pressure.[17]

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
paragraphs.text: Transparency is essential. Providers should be encouraged within clear regulatory standards and guidelines that define acceptable and unacceptable practices, to act ethically, to disclose the logic behind their algorithmic design, and to demonstrate that their services do not harm or unfairly influence children. This reinforces the duty of companies to respect and uphold children's rights in digital spaces. This approach is grounded in General Comment No. 25 of the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child, which calls on States to require all businesses that affect children's rights in the digital environment to adhere to the highest standards of ethics, privacy and safety throughout the design, development and marketing of their products and services.[18]

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE
paragraphs.text: Given the structural asymmetries in knowledge, power and access to information between companies and users, it is unreasonable to expect vulnerable users to prove harm caused by complex algorithms or manipulative design. A lack of transparency in digital systems makes it nearly impossible for consumers to gather evidence.[19] For that reason accountability must begin upstream, with those who design and profit from digital environments. This reinforces the need for ex ante obligations that proactively ban harmful design features, such as addictive design, and enforce accountability.[20]

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE
paragraphs.text: Moreover, the rapid pace of change in the digital environment means that legislation is often one step behind. By placing ethical responsibility on companies in advance, requiring them to assess and prevent foreseeable harms, the law can help bridge the gap between regulation and innovation, ensuring that fairness is not retrofitted but embedded from the outset.

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE
paragraphs.text: Digital service providers should therefore be required to proactively demonstrate that their design choices and monetization strategies are safe, fair and developmentally appropriate for minors before releasing them to the public.

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE
paragraphs.text: DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN IS LID VAN ECPAT INTERNATIONAL

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE
paragraphs.text: This approach is aligned with the precautionary principle, which is recognised in both EU consumer policy and children’s rights law. As articulated in General Comment No. 25 of the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child, children must be protected even when conclusive scientific evidence of harm is not yet available, particularly in the face of emerging technologies. The precautionary principle calls for preventive safeguards, not reactive fixes, and places the burden of evidence on those who introduce risks and, in this case, digital businesses.

section_path: CHILDREN'S RIGHTS IN THE DIGITAL WORLD
BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE PRECAUTIONARY PRINCIPLE
paragraphs.text: Reversing the burden of proof would not only reflect a child-rights-based approach under Articles 3 (best interests of the child as a primary consideration), 6 (right to life, survival and development) and 24 (right to health) of the UNCRC, but also facilitate effective enforcement.

Children remain exposed to manipulative design features because existing EU legislation and regulations do not clearly prohibit many harmful practices. The prohibition of dark patterns under the DSA applies only to online platforms, and the UCPD lists only a few examples and none referring specifically to digital interfaces. This fragmented approach creates uncertainty for both users and businesses about what is permitted, including monetised gaming features such as loot boxes or in-app currencies, which remain inconsistently regulated across Member States.

The DFA should therefore include explicit prohibitions of harmful design practices, clear guidance on the protection of children from unfair commercial practices, a ban on dark patterns and a listing concrete examples. This would provide legal certainty for platforms and empower regulators, by giving them clear tools to audit and intervene to take consistent action.

From a child rights perspective, such specificity is essential as children must be protected from exploitation. Clearly identifying and banning manipulative digital practices that target minors would give that obligation real legal force.

Finally, an ecosystem approach is needed to ensure effective enforcement and legal clarity across the digital value chain. Protecting children’s rights in the digital environment requires shared responsibility, not only from the direct service providers but also from the broader ecosystem that enables and profits from children’s online activity. App stores, online marketplaces and payment providers should therefore be required to contribute to compliance and accountability efforts, ensuring that children’s rights and well-being are upheld across every layer of the digital environment. Such shared responsibility would strengthen oversight, close enforcement gaps, and promote a coherent and fair digital market.

Clear and well-defined rules and guidelines enable effective enforcement. Effective supervision requires enforceability by design. Regulators need precise, practical tools to intervene, and companies need predictable obligations that facilitate compliance
Hart voor Kinderrechten
DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN
DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN IS LID VAN ECPAP INTERNATIONAL
X

without reducing protection. Above all, there must be strong and coordinated oversight to ensure that children's rights are upheld in practice, not only in principle. Ultimately, the DFA should aim to eliminate ambiguity in how consumer law applies to digital practices, providing clarity for both users and businesses and enabling enforcement bodies to act decisively.

Digital tools and services, when designed responsibly, can enrich children's development and civic participation. However, many digital services "are currently designed for and by adults", meaning children's presence often goes unrecognized or unaccommodated on the platforms they use. The DFA represents a crucial opportunity to align the European Union's digital market legal framework with the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child.

A child-centred DFA must therefore prohibit manipulative and addictive design practices, require fairness and ethics by design, and ensure that companies bear the burden of proving that their systems are safe, fair and non-exploitative. It must provide legal clarity through explicit rules, guarantee enforceability through coordinated oversight, and uphold the precautionary principle to protect children even when evidence of harm is still emerging.

By embedding children's rights and the protection, participation and development of children at its core, the DFA can close the gaps left by existing frameworks and create a digital environment that is safe, transparent and empowering for every child.
Hart voor Kinderrechten
DEFENCE FOR CHILDREN
